data.title: Compañía de Minas Buenaventura S.A.A. 2024 Q4 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Compañía de Minas Buenaventura S.A.A. in conjunction with their 2024 Q4 earnings call.

data.title: Barclays: The 100% Rally Is Just The Beginning Of Greater Gains
data.description:
Barclays (BCS) stock has been on a tear over the past year. Having more than doubled in value, the shares have also already soared 15% in value in the YTD, building with a further gain since our previous coverage in the New Year. On the back of a satisfactory set of Q4 results as well, we see further upside brewing for the Blue Eagle bank.

Q4 Results Recap

While Barclays did beat consensus estimates on its top line (£6.71 billion), with revenue up 24.4% to £6.96 billion, it was the bottom line and dividend miss that sparked its earnings day pullback. Nonetheless, the seemingly conservative nature of management on the earnings call was later digested by investors as a positive, which then spurred a rebound in the following days.

Group NII rose 11.5% to £3.50 billion, thanks to a strong showing in both the UK's retail and corporate side, as the higher NIM from the acquisition of Tesco (OTCPK:TSCDF) Bank and structural hedges served as tailwinds. Group FCOI spiked 40.9% to £3.46 billion, as IB's trading income leaped 66.7%, while UK's FCOI posted a three-fold increment, also thanks to Tesco Bank.

Cost was the disappointment, however. Although operating costs fell 10.4% to £4.24 billion, it still came short of consensus' £4.11 billion, and not for the first time. This was mostly due to Tesco driving UK costs up by 1.5%, and slower cost efficiencies in the company's IB arm. Along with this, impairments were also higher against consensus, driven by Tesco's lower credit quality book.

As a result, the head start from the beat on revenue was watered down by the higher costs, leading to a pre-tax profit (PBT) of £1.66 billion and attributable profit of £965 million. This resulted in basic EPS also falling short of consensus' 7.0p, at 6.7p, not helped by a miss on consensus' final DPS (5.5p vs. 5.7p), despite the announcement of an additional £1 billion in buybacks.

Nevertheless, Barclays' underlying metrics remain promising. Deposits jumped 4.1% to £560.70 billion, and have bottomed sooner than the board had expected, while loans grew 3.8% to £414.50 billion. Therefore, group equity also stepped up by 0.9% to £72.48 billion, aiding Barclays' book value, with TNAV per share also ticking up 7.9% to a handsome 357p.

A Costly Bet?

On that note, it's the TNAV per share that we really want to focus on. The firm continues to trade below its TNAV, and this is one of the main pillars for our investment thesis. It's also our counterargument to detractors highlighting the rampant run up in the share price and calling it a peak; we hold the belief that the stock remains undervalued, and is in fact, still a safe bet today.

It's no secret that both Barclays stock and the wider banking industry have been suffering from a lack of multiple expansion over the past decade or so. Initial scepticism surrounding income growth with sub-1% interest rates were reversed over the past few years, but this fear was then replaced with higher impairments potentially impacting earnings－but this hasn't come to fruition.

This risk-shifting game has now reverted back to one of lower income growth, however, as interest rates come back down. But, this misses the point. For one, banks like Barclays are renewing huge swathes of their structural hedges at much higher rates. Secondly, a return to sub-1% rates is unlikely in our view, as we don't see CPI returning to sub-2% until 2026/27.

For those reasons, our belief is that this will support income growth (more on this in the preceding segment). But more importantly, the higher ups are taking control of what they can, and the costs. Barclays' cost:income ratio (C:I) has been coming down substantially. And with an FY26 target of high 50% from today's 65.9%, this is something we're cautiously excited by.

In conjunction with cost efficiencies, CEO C.S. Venkat's pledge to return c.£10 billion to shareholders from FY24 through to FY26 further enriches our thesis too, as we see plenty of opportunities for EPS enrichment. The preference of buybacks over dividends will be a huge positive for the stock's intrinsic value from a TNAV and EPS point of view. And with £7.0 billion left, upside persists.

Room for Income to Soar

That being said, what really excites us is the two-sided uplift to EPS, with plenty of income growth opportunities as well. With the team guiding for revenue of c.£30 billion by FY26, this would mark c.11.9% growth from FY24, therefore dispelling the narrative of lower rates equating to lower bottom-line growth. But where do we anticipate this to come from?

We imagine a good chunk of it will come from the UK, with robust growth in the pipeline. With higher NIMs, larger deposit inflows, and the propensity of Tesco Bank customers to spend more, these should serve as catalysts and offset the temporary opex pressures over the medium term. In fact, there may even be room to leverage on Tesco's and Barclays's connections beyond FY26.

"We talked when we bought Tesco about leveraging the capability that we have from US cards and bringing it to the UK. You're now seeing that both in terms of Avios and Tesco and Amazon. So, more of a partnership focus in the UK that really helps us grow and diversify that cards business." - CFO Anna Cross

The path for both UKC and PBWM is also decent. We envision deposit outflows to reverse meaningfully as inflation returns to normal levels, and this should offset the impact of rate cuts on NIMs, with the aid of structural hedges. What's more, investments in PBWM's digital and lending propositions should drive FCOI growth, boosted by higher transactional activity from lower rates.

Even so, IB is where we see the bulk of growth coming from, especially in the BFU segment, where Barclays has been growing its market share. We're also convinced that lower rates will benefit activity over the medium term, with a further build on advisory. Additionally, ICB should steadily grow along with the GM sub-division, from higher balances and a stabilising bond market.

Meanwhile, the non-renewal of Barclays' partnership with American Airlines (AAL) has shaken USC sentiment. Still, we remain upbeat on the bank's US card business. The onboarding of GM's (GM) card portfolio should partially offset some of the outflows from AA's decoupling. Paired with lower inflation to come, and we estimate inflows to return with higher spending by FY26.

Valuation and Projections

Looking forward to Q1, though, we're cautiously optimistic. We're anticipating seasonal deposit outflows from tax payments. That said, higher NIMs from Tesco Bank and structural hedges should still allow NII to tick up sequentially. Combined with the guided run rate of >£250m on UK FCOI thanks to Tesco Bank customers, we forecast UK income rising c.14.7% (Y/Y) to £2.10 billion.

We're estimating similar deposit/NIM effects on UKC and PBWM, but have still pencilled in their respective incomes rising to £485 million and £353 million from a declining rate environment. Having said that, we have IB income down by 4.7% to £3.17 billion, weighed down by a lacklustre GM showing due to elevated 10Y yields, and a flat BFU, if YTD Dealogic data is anything to go by.

USC is our most uncertain estimate, but we think new retail savings products should aid inflows and offset the potential drop-off in NIM ever so slightly. All in all, we still believe group income will expand by c.3.0% to £6.95 billion in Q1. Moreover, we project Barclays's C:I ratio to dip to 57.2%, which should result in basic EPS of 11.4p, pushed further up by buybacks.

Hence, we have a good build for FY25, and also beyond. We've projected revenue to grow at a healthy but fairly conservative CAGR of c.5.4% through to FY27. As a consequence of both income growth and cost efficiencies, we're forecasting an EPS CAGR of c.18.3% as well, with FY25 EPS at 40.7p, FY26 at 49.2p, and FY27 at 57.0p－a tinge below current consensus.

Thus, by taking a weighted combination-of-multiples approach, we have a price target of 385p ($19.40) for Barclays stock. This is achieved by averaging Barclays's key earnings ratios to their historical and sector(s) medians, which include the likes of Lloyds (LYG), Deutsche (DB), and JPMorgan (JPM) among many others.

Price Model Barclays Sector AVG 5Y AVG
P/B 0.7 1.1 0.4
P/E 8.9 10.0 8.1
FP/E 7.5 10.6 9.6
FPEG 0.5 0.8 0.4
Price Target 385p // $19.40

Verdict and Rating

The upside potential remains sizable in our opinion, in spite of the rapid rise the stock has experienced over the past year or so. This multiple expansion has been long overdue, and we hold the view that a higher multiple is warranted for the FTSE 100 (UKX) stalwart's earnings potential and bulk of shareholder returns to come.

Operating costs will be a slight headwind in FY25, but we're projecting this to fall going into FY26 as more synergies and scalability from lower rates are realised alongside productivity and cost efficiencies. The UK business has room to expand from the acquisition of Tesco Bank on both the NII and FCOI front, whilst PBWM has sturdy momentum coming from higher client activity.

IB is also showing green shoots and promise with declining borrowing costs, as well as market share gains in BFU. At the same time, USC should reap the benefits of lower rates from increased card spending and a solid credit portfolio. On that basis, and even with our relatively conservative estimates, we reiterate our BUY rating with a price target of 385p ($19.40).

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Alibaba: Why I Am More Bullish Than Ever
data.description:
Alibaba Group Holding Limited (NYSE:BABA) saw solid growth in the vital e-Commerce and Cloud segments in the December quarter, highlighting that the Chinese company is still very much a growth play. Alibaba submitted better-than-expected earnings and revenue, mainly because of a reacceleration of growth in the domestic e-Commerce business. The company generated a ton of free cash flow as well, and returned a high percentage of its FCF to shareholders in the form of stock buybacks. Shares remain widely undervalued, based off of earnings, in my opinion, and I see long-term revaluation potential for Alibaba.

Previous rating

In my last work on Alibaba, I discussed the Chinese company’s bid to stay competitive in the Cloud business by launching its own AI model, dubbed Qwen 2.5-Max: The AI Race Is Heating Up. Alibaba’s tech investments have the potential to help Alibaba diversify away from its e-Commerce focused operating portfolio and potentially help the company accelerate its top-line growth as well. The e-Commerce segment also did very well in the December quarter and with shares continuing to trade at a very low P/E ratio, I believe Alibaba has potential to grow into a higher valuation multiplier.

Alibaba’s Q3 2025 beat estimates

Alibaba generated better-than-expected earnings for its third fiscal quarter: the e-Commerce company reported $2.95 per-share in adjusted earnings, out-performing the average prediction by $0.29 per-share. The company also generated $38.6B in revenue, beating the consensus estimate by $351M.

Alibaba benefited chiefly from an acceleration of top-line growth in its vitally important domestic e-Commerce business, known as Taobao and Tmall Group. Here, the e-Commerce platform generated 136.1B Chinese Yuan ($18.6B) in revenue, showing 5% growth year-over-year.

Since Taobao and Tmall Group generated only 1% growth here in 2Q25, Alibaba's core e-Commerce segment benefited from a 4 PP revenue growth acceleration. In total, Alibaba generated 280.2B Chinese Yuan ($38.4B) revenue, showing 8% growth year-over-year (+3 PP Q/Q).

Because of the roll-out of a new AI model, Alibaba is seeing accelerating growth in its core Cloud platform as well. Here, Alibaba generated 31.7B Chinese Yuan ($4.3B) in revenue, showing 13% growth year-over-year.

As a result of accelerating growth in Taobao and Tmall Group and Cloud, as well as consistently strong growth in Alibaba's international e-Commerce business AIDC (+32% Y/Y revenue growth), the company benefited from surging profitability… which caused Alibaba's share price to soar 8% after earnings. Alibaba's net income surged 333% Y/Y to 46.4B Chinese Yuan ($6.4B) and the company's free cash flow also shows a lot of promise…

Strong free cash flow performance and path to $20B in annual free cash flow

The main reason why I like Alibaba relates to the company being highly free cash flow-profitable. In fact, Alibaba is one of the most profitable, large-cap Chinese companies in terms of free cash flow, which is largely due to Alibaba being a dominant force in the domestic e-Commerce industry.

In the third fiscal quarter, Alibaba generated 39.0B Chinese Yuan ($5.3B) in free cash flow. While the company's free cash flow declined 31% Y/Y, mainly due to higher CapEx, Alibaba had a high free cash flow margin of 14%. In the year-earlier period, the e-Commerce enterprise had a free cash flow margin of 21%. At the current rate of free cash flow growth, Alibaba is on track to achieve ~$20B in annualized FCF this calendar year.

The e-Commerce platform also continued to return a lot of cash flow to shareholders in the December quarter, which further underscores Alibaba’s value as a capital return play. In 3Q25, Alibaba spent 9.1B Chinese Yuan ($1.3B) on stock buybacks. In the past two years, Alibaba spent an average of $3.2B on stock buybacks, and I expect more stock buybacks in the quarters to come.

Alibaba's valuation makes no sense

Chinese e-Commerce companies became favorites for investors in September 2024, which is when Beijing announced a major stimulus package to support domestic lending and consumption. However, the hype around the stimulus package, which I discussed here, was short-lived and fizzled out by November… which is when valuation valuations of e-Commerce companies like Alibaba, PDD Holdings (PDD) and JD.com (JD) became highly attractive again.

Although valuations of Chinese e-Commerce companies have surged again in 2025 on prospects of stronger economic growth and continual fiscal support from Beijing, shares of Alibaba are still undervalued, in my opinion.

Alibaba is valued at a forward P/E ratio of 14X, while its main domestic e-Commerce rivals PDD Holdings and JD.com are trading at a forward price-to-earnings ratios of 8.1X and 8.5X. I like PDD Holdings chiefly because of its Temu-related growth potential while JD.com is also more of a capital play for investors, in my opinion.

I believe Alibaba's shares could easily revalue higher given that the company's key segments (e-Commerce, Cloud, AIDC) are all seeing strengthening growth, resulting in a better profitability profile for Alibaba. The Chinese e-Commerce company could revalue to a ~20X P/E ratio… under the condition that Alibaba can continue to grow its earnings rapidly, while expanding its Cloud business through the lever of AI (models), and maintaining double-digit free cash flow margins. A 20X P/E ratio, which would also be justified by Alibaba's enormous free cash flow strength and focus on stock buybacks, implies a fair value for the firm's shares in the neighborhood of $195 per-share. This is my long-term price target for Alibaba.

Risks with Alibaba

Alibaba’s biggest risk is its dependence on the e-Commerce market, which still generates about two-thirds (~62%, including AIDC) of the company’s total revenue. In the future, Alibaba will have to diversify away from its commerce-centric platform model and open up new lines of business. It is Cloud that, I believe, has the most potential to drive significant top-line growth in the future. If Alibaba fails to build out its tech platforms (Cloud) and diversify its revenue streams, investors may see valuation headwinds.

Final thoughts

Alibaba’s third fiscal quarter earnings report card was very good, with the e-Commerce company seeing an acceleration in its top-line growth and promising momentum in the important Taobao and Tmall Group as well as the Cloud segment. Alibaba's accelerating growth also had a positive impact on Alibaba's profitability profile, and the company is now on track to $20B in annual free cash flow. Alibaba has seen a strong price increase lately, but shares remain underpriced given the firm's underlying free cash flow strength. I believe Alibaba is, and will remain, a top growth play for investors seeking exposure to the Chinese market. Shares are undervalued and continue to have a ton of revaluation potential.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Elon Musk's Quest For A 'Super App'
data.description:
Elon Musk, X & the Quest for a “Super App”:

Elon Musk has long been fascinated by the idea of a “super app” - a platform that integrates messaging, payments, and financial services into a single ecosystem. This vision dates back to 1999 when he co-founded X.com, an online bank that later merged with Confinity to form PayPal. After PayPal’s $1.5 billion sale to eBay in 2002, Musk used his proceeds to launch SpaceX and Tesla.

Now, inspired by China’s Alipay, WeChat Pay, and UnionPay, Musk is looking to transform X into a financial powerhouse with X Money. These Chinese platforms seamlessly blend social networking, commerce, and digital payments. When Musk acquired Twitter for $44 billion in 2022, one of his long-term goals was to integrate similar financial capabilities into the platform.

However, moving money is a heavily regulated industry, requiring state-by-state licensing. Over the past year, X has been steadily acquiring money transmitter licenses to build a nationwide financial network. According to FinCEN (Financial Crimes Enforcement Network), X has now secured approval for money transfers in 41 states, with its US money transfer service expected to launch in the first quarter of 2025.

Initially, X Money will allow users to send funds to others on the platform, while creators can accept payments and store funds in an X digital wallet, much like Venmo or Cash App. Once the digital wallet is established, Musk’s ambitions extend far beyond simple transactions with future offerings projected to include high-yield money market accounts, stock trading, and digital currency transactions.

On January 28, 2025, X CEO Linda Yaccarino announced a partnership with Visa to accelerate these financial goals. The deal will enable instant funding via Visa Direct, allowing users to instantly transfer money, make payments with a linked debit card, and move funds between X Money and traditional bank accounts. With Visa, the world’s largest payment network, X is positioning itself as a serious player in peer-to-peer (P2P) and account-to-account (A2A) payments - competing directly with Zelle, Venmo, and Cash App.

But the road ahead won’t be easy. X’s ambitions put it in direct competition with both big tech and financial giants. Apple, for example, has long been accused of blocking the rise of “super apps” to protect its own ecosystem of services. Regulators have been scrutinizing Apple’s alleged anti-competitive practices since 2017. A recent DOJ anti-trust lawsuit against Apple revealed internal concerns that a super app could weaken consumer reliance on the iPhone’s software and payment system. The broader market is also shifting. Facebook has expanded its financial offerings by integrating shopping, gaming, and dating, while consumers increasingly use multiple platforms for communication, payments, and entertainment.

While X Money is an ambitious step toward Musk’s vision, creating a financial “super app” in the US remains an uphill battle. Competition is fierce, regulatory hurdles are high, and consumer adoption is uncertain. Whether Musk can succeed, where others have struggled, remains to be seen.

data.title: Solaris Energy Infrastructure, Inc. 2024 Q4 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Solaris Energy Infrastructure, Inc. in conjunction with their 2024 Q4 earnings call.

data.title: IsoEnergy Is Too Richly Priced
data.description:
Uranium exploration and development company, IsoEnergy Ltd. (OTCQX:ISENF) has been a great investment bet over the last five years, despite being a pre-revenue firm. With returns on the rise, and hopes that the firm can achieve profitability, I have come to analyse this firm. However, my analysis suggests that the road to profitability has too many risks to justify the large premium to intrinsic value that the share price has.

The Business Model

IsoEnergy has a fairly simple business model: the company buys, explores, and develops uranium mineral properties in Canada, the United States, and Australia. It is an early-stage, pre-revenue company and so, investors should already expect that the company has a negative intrinsic value. The firm’s market valuation will represent the present value of growth opportunities (PVGO) as assessed by market participants.

The success of the business depends on its ability to identify and develop high-grade uranium deposits, with a long-term view toward turning those assets into productive assets or generating sufficient acquisition interest from larger industry players.

At present, IsoEnergy’s key assets include the high-grade Hurricane uranium deposit in the Athabasca Basin, Saskatchewan, and past-producing mines in Utah.

Quality Assets are a Source of Competitive Advantage

The quality and grade of IsoEnergy’s uranium deposits are one of the company’s key strengths. The Hurricane deposit holds an impressive 48.6 million pounds of U3O8 at an average grade of 34.5%, giving IsoEnergy a significant advantage in a market where lower-grade deposits often prove economically unviable. The Hurricane deposit, along with IsoEnergy’s diversified portfolio of exploration-stage and past-producing assets, have a lot of upside potential.

At the end of the 2023 fiscal year, IsoEnergy concluded a share-for-share merger with Consolidated Uranium Inc., expanding its asset base to include past-producing uranium mines in Utah that are ready to be restarted under favorable market conditions. Consequently, the company’s strategic positioning was strengthened, giving IsoEnergy both near-term production potential and long-term growth opportunities.

In addition, because of its location in the Athabasca Basin, which has some of the richest uranium deposits in the world, IsoEnergy has access to established infrastructure, experienced labor, and a regulatory environment familiar with uranium mining, further improving its strategic positioning. Moreover, IsoEnergy’s financial backing from NexGen Energy Ltd., which holds a 32.9% stake, gives it financial stability and strategic guidance, which are both important for investor confidence, even though, admittedly, they are not sustainable competitive advantages.

Share Dilution is a Real Risk

As the table below shows, IsoEnergy’s net operating profit after tax (NOPAT) has declined from -$4 million in 2020 to -$14.7 million in the trailing twelve months (TTM).

Economic Category (Values in millions)

Source: Author calculations and company filings

This deepening lack of core profitability means that the firm is heavily reliant on external financing to fund its exploration and development activities. Given the need to issue equity and have private placements to fund core activities, shareholders are exposed to potential dilution. In fact, the company has grown its number of shares from 87 million in 2020 to 166 million in the TTM.

IsoEnergy’s ROIC is Improving

There are some positive signs: the company has improved its return on invested capital (ROIC) from -6.2% in 2020 to -4.3% in the TTM, thanks to growing balance sheet efficiency. This is probably what has been responsible for the share price improvement in the last five years, with the share price rising nearly 446%. Markets reward firms who improve ROIC in excess of expectations.

Exploration is Always Risky

Exploration is about probabilities. Finding deposits is not guaranteed. Aside from geological uncertainties, there are also risks from permit delays to capital expenditures that run higher than what was forecast. For a company that does not have a producing asset, anything that slows down the path to profitability is particularly challenging, especially when there is a risk that the company may miss favorable uranium market conditions when it does push its assets into production.

Uranium Prices Are Volatile

As the chart below highlights, uranium prices are highly volatile, and have only shown a modest long-term tendency toward higher prices. High prices tend to be short-lived, and this is true even now, in the midst of a global energy crisis. Although the current global outlook is positive, given rising demand for nuclear energy, history suggests that high prices will not stay. In fact, the current global price is 26% down from the January high. This makes planning very hard, and puts pressure on miners to operate low cost operations. The challenge for IsoEnergy is that interest rate and overall costs of capital are on the rise, as well as inflation. So not only must the company face higher costs of capital and input, it must do so while developing low-cost operations.

Adding to the company’s challenges is that the Canadian, and US governments are particularly stringent when it comes to uranium, given the environmental concerns, and this can make getting a permit a lengthy and unpredictable process. Although the Trump Administration may be friendlier to nuclear energy, historically, nuclear has not been able to take off in the country, there is nothing yet to show that the industry will benefit, although looser regulations may make it easier to obtain permits.

It should be said that IsoEnergy has the advantage that its high-grade uranium deposits should give it lower processing costs, increasing the chances that they prove economically viable. Furthermore, there is some financial flexibility from having a partner such as NexGen Energy, although share dilution will still remain a large and real risk. Finally, the firm’s diverse asset base gives it multiple growth pathways and production opportunities, reducing the risks associated with any single project.

There are Upsides

There are reasons to believe that IsoEnergy’s valuation should continue to increase. Although uranium prices are volatile, energy security concerns, and global decarbonization efforts, should lead to a long-run increase in demand for nuclear energy, pushing uranium prices higher. This would improve the economic viability of IsoEnergy’s projects and maybe even hasten their path to production.

The company’s support by NexGen and its growing asset base, may make it an attractive acquisition target. If a major uranium player wants to consolidate assets in the Athabasca Basin, IsoEnergy’s high-grade deposits would command a premium.

As I discussed earlier, with likely more favorable regulations and increased federal government support for nuclear energy or streamlined permitting processes, IsoEnergy’s operational risks will come down. In addition, with advances in small modular reactors (SMRs) and next-generation nuclear technology, long-term uranium supply costs may fall, making economic viability easier to attain.

Finally, a discovery of more high-grade uranium assets could significantly enhance IsoEnergy’s valuation, given that high-grade discoveries are in such demand. The probability of this is increased by the fact that they are already operating in a high-grade region.

Yes, Intrinsic Valuation Is Negative

As I signaled early on, the company’s intrinsic value is negative. Take IsoEnergy’s NOPAT for the TTM period, capitalize it by its 5.81% cost of capital, and add the value of its net deferred tax liabilities (DTLs), and you will get an intrinsic value of -$257.2 million, or -$1.44 per share, compared to a share price of $1.75 at the time of writing. This simply reflects the pre-revenue nature of the business. As such, investors must weigh whether the $3.19/share premium that reflects the firm’s PVGO is too large or not. To my mind, an investor who wants exposure to this risky market should demand a much smaller premium. If the share price was reduced by $1 per share, for example, that would be a more reasonable premium for me. At this price, the PVGO is too high.

Conclusion

Valuing pre-revenue firms is all about assessing the achievability of the firm’s profitability. While IsoEnergy has quality, high-grade assets, the share dilution, volatile uranium prices, and historic difficulties with scaling uranium in North America, mean that I would like to see a much smaller premium on its share price. At this price, there are just too many risks and too little reward.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Fresnillo: An Overlooked Large Gold And Silver Producer
data.description:
Introduction

As gold and silver prices remain very strong, I wanted to see if I could gain some exposure "off the beaten path." While most large North American gold producers are well known and are trading at premium valuations, I wanted to follow up on Fresnillo plc (OTCPK:FNLPF). Fresnillo is a very large gold and silver producer with its primary listing in London, and this Mexico-focused company generally stays under the radar.

As you can see above, the company also has some large projects in the advanced exploration stage with Orisyvo coming in at almost 10 million ounces of gold and some silver while Guanajuato predominantly is a silver project with a quarter of a billion ounces of silver. As gold and silver prices are currently high, and the company is generating strong cash flows, Fresnillo should be in a position to fund its expansion plans themselves.

Fresnillo has its main listing on the London Stock Exchange where it's trading with FRES as its ticker symbol. The average daily volume in London is almost 1 million shares and the current market capitalization is approximately 5.8B GBP. Using the current exchange rate of 1.266 USD per GBP, the market cap in USD is approximately $7.3B.

One of the largest precious metals producers in the world

The Mexican government hasn’t really had a pro-mining stance in the past few years and this culminated in the fight to get an open-pit mining ban approved. Fortunately, the new president seems to have a more pragmatic approach and the first new permits (including open pit permits) started to flow again with Grupo México and Alamos Gold recently reporting the approval of their expansion projects. While those approvals were predominantly add-ons to existing mines (and amendments to existing permits) and we're still waiting to see major new mines to be permitted in the country, it's encouraging to see the administration is at least considering files.

While we still haven’t seen financial results, Fresnillo has already reported the production results for 2024. The company’s production came in at just over 630,000 ounces of gold (which was a guidance beat) thanks to the higher grade ore at Fresnillo and higher volumes mined and processed at San Julian and Saucito. Fresnillo ended the year on a very strong note as it produced in excess of 200,000 ounces of gold in the final quarter of the year, which means full-year results should reflect very nice gold revenue as the strongest production quarter coincided with a higher gold price.

Fresnillo also is a large silver producer as it produced just over 56 million ounces of silver in 2024, which was pretty flat compared to last year with the production coming in at the lower end of the guidance.

While precious metals definitely are the company’s focus, it also produces decent amounts of lead (almost 150 million pounds) and zinc (in excess of 250 million pounds). These by-product credits help to keep the production cost for the gold and silver under control.

As mentioned, Fresnillo still has to report on its full-year financial results, but at least we can look back at the H1 results and try to draw up a comparison from there.

Fresnillo reported total revenue of almost $1.5B in the first half of the year, on which it generated almost $400M in gross profit. After also deducting the overhead expenses as well as the expensed exploration activities, the EBIT was approximately $211M, excluding the impact of the revaluation of Silverstream.

There was a positive contribution of the Silverstream contract to the tune of $66.5M. This is related to a historical agreement with Penoles whereby Fresnillo is entitled to the silver sold from the Sabinas mine. As Fresnillo made an upfront cash payment and is making ongoing payments per ounce of silver, the value of this agreement fluctuates along with silver prices. A higher silver price makes it more valuable, and a lower silver prices makes it less valuable. Considering the Silverstream revaluations are an unrealized asset value update, I do not take it into consideration when looking at Fresnillo’s profits.

This means Fresnillo reported a net income of approximately $71M of which $32.1M was attributable to its own shareholders. This represented an EPS of US$0.044.

Looking at the cash flow result, Fresnillo reported an operating cash flow of almost $548M before changes in the working capital, but we should of course also deduct the $72M in taxes and employee profit-sharing payments.

There are a few additional adjustments to make: Fresnillo recorded a $19.2M interest income in its investing cash flow and a $24.1M interest payment in its financing activities. On top of that, the $13.7M contribution from the Silverstream contract is booked as an investing cash flow but really is an operating cash flow for all intents and purposes.

This means the adjusted operating cash flow was approximately $480M after also deducting the $3.3M in lease payments and approximately $455M after making a final adjustment to the tax element (the taxes owed were higher than the taxes paid).

As the image above shows, the total capex was just $170M, resulting in a net free cash flow result of $285M. Assuming $40M was paid (or will have to be paid) to the non-controlling interests, the net free cash flow attributable to Fresnillo was approximately $245M. Divided over the current share count of 737M shares, this represents an FCFPS of $0.33 per share.

While those results don’t sound too impressive, keep in mind Fresnillo’s H2 gold production was approximately 20% higher than the production volume in the first half of the year. Additionally, the H2 metals prices were higher than the $27.6/oz for silver and the $2170/oz for gold. This means the company’s financial performance will be superior in the second half of the year. I anticipate the higher gold production and higher gold price to have added at least US$225M in after-tax cash flow, but the H2 capex will also be approximately $70M higher. This would bring the full-year attributable free cash flow to approximately $625-650M, for a result of $0.86 per share.

What will 2025 bring?

The company also released its production guidance for 2025, and unfortunately, the output will decrease a bit. As you can see below, the midpoint of the silver production is just 52.5 million ounces while the midpoint of the gold production comes in at around 550,000 ounces.

While that's a bummer, it's not unexpected as Fresnillo is quite transparent on its three-year production plan (the table above also shows the anticipated production volumes for 2026 and 2027). Fortunately, the high gold and silver prices may allow Fresnillo to keep its operating cash flow result at a slightly higher level than in 2024, but unfortunately, as you can see below, the company also plans to spend $80M more on sustaining capex.

Investment thesis

This means I don’t expect any spectacular profit increases in 2025 notwithstanding the higher gold and silver price. The lower production volumes in combination with a higher capex will likely limit the financial performance, but I do think the company has a shot at keeping its net free cash flow stable in the $0.85-0.90/share range. This would represent 65-70 pence per share at the current exchange rate.

The balance sheet is exceptionally robust and although Fresnillo had a net debt of just around $200M as of the end of June 2024, I expect the company to have ended 2024 with a net cash position (subject to working capital changes). And that makes it an interesting gold and silver pick for me.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Micron: Large Upside And Strong Momentum
data.description:
Executive Summary

I assign a Strong Buy on Micron Technology, Inc. (NASDAQ:MU) because the stock appears deeply undervalued. I believe the company is well managed, and its fundamental strength is powered by management's consistent focus on business expansion. Micron is extremely successful in navigating a challenging competitive environment, which is powered by an aggressive focus on innovation. Offering cutting-edge products of high demand helps MU generate premium profitability and maintain healthy capital allocation approaches.

Company Analysis

Micron focuses on developing innovative memory and storage solutions. The company offers a diverse portfolio of high-performance DRAM, NAND, and NOR memory products under its "Micron" and "Crucial" brands.

The company's largest business unit is CNBU, which generated 50% of the total revenue during the latest reportable quarter. This is the most profitable segment with a solid 39% operating margin during the quarter ended November 28, 2024. As we can also see from the below disclosure, this is also the fastest-growing segment together with SBU.

The rapid revenue growth demonstrated by CNBU and SBU is explained by the exposure of these segments to the thriving data center industry. The picture below is beautiful because the global data center market is experiencing robust growth due to the ongoing AI revolution.

Micron's largest and highly likely the toughest competitor in data centers is a South Korean giant called Samsung Electronics Co., Ltd. (OTCPK:SSNLF). This is a formidable competitor which represents 22% of South Korean GDP. There are also several smaller competitors like SK Hynix, Western Digital, and Seagate.

Despite fierce competition, Micron's market positioning is robust, which is powered by the company's relentless commitment to innovation. The company's R&D budget is constantly expanding, which means that the management prioritizes innovation over maximizing profits at all costs. This is a long-term approach, which certainly increases the chances of accumulating value for shareholders over the long haul.

As a result, Micron is actively innovating across the entire portfolio of its offerings. The company released several new products in 2024, and there were reports that in March 2024 the company already sold most of the supply for 2025. Such a high demand for Micron's HBM (High Bandwidth Memory) offerings underscores the company's strong positioning in the industry. The company takes legal protection of its intellectual property very seriously as the company is currently among the Top-15 U.S. patent assignees, ahead of behemoths like Microsoft and Amazon.

The company's financial health, which is crucial in maintaining its innovation capacity, is indisputable. Micron demonstrates excellent operating profitability with high and expanding EBIT and EBITDA margins. The bottom-line profitability is also impressive compared to the sector median and historical averages. The management's capital allocation approach also looks sound as the company ended the previous quarter with $7.6 billion in cash and low leverage.

Let's summarize the analysis of internal factors. Micron's business is no doubt well-rounded as it leverages a diversified business mix, focuses on innovations, stellar profitability, and sound capital allocation. With all these positives in mind, we can move to external factors.

The business environment looks extremely favorable due to the thriving data center industry powered by AI tailwinds. According to Mordor Intelligence, the HBM market will deliver a 25%+ CAGR by 2030. I think that this is not an unrealistic projection as the data center boom continues. According to The Economic Times, tech giants plan to invest more than $300 billion in AI and data centers during 2025.

Among important external factors, promising partnerships also add optimism. During the CES 2025 event, Nvidia's CEO emphasized the critical role of Micron in powering the required HBM capacity for innovative RTX 50 series chips. There are also partnerships with other key semiconductor players like Broadcom, Intel, AMD, and Dell.

The market's sentiment around Micron is strong in 2025, as the stock significantly outperforms the S&P 500 index. Positive sentiment together with accumulating share price momentum are the two other robust bullish reasons to invest in MU.

The Discount Is Massive

Wall Street analysts expect aggressive EPS growth over the next eleven quarters, meaning that I can expect strong free cash flow growth for my discounted cash flow model. The next eleven quarters are almost three full fiscal years, and the EPS CAGR is projected to be 42.3%.

The year 1 FCF assumption is $6.02 billion, which I calculated by myself. My calculations are based on the TTM operating cash flow, which delivered a 36% margin. With the help of revenue forecasts from consensus, I figure out the FY2026 operating cash flow projection. To finalize calculations with the FCF estimate, I also deduct capex.

The discount rate is 9.60%, and it is a reliable assumption since it is based on a reputable CAPM approach.

As I mentioned, the EPS CAGR for the next three years is 42.3%. This is a very aggressive growth rate, which I prefer to soften significantly. Therefore, I use this growth rate only for year 2. For the years 3-5 I expect the growth to decelerate by 10 percentage points per year. The perpetual growth rate is a conservative 2%.

The upside potential is 56%, according to my financial model. The fair value of the company is $180 billion, meaning that there is a massive discount.

Potential Drawbacks

The entire semiconductor industry is highly cyclical, especially in the memory chip segment. As a result, Micron's revenue growth was extremely volatile over the last decade, which is outlined below. Such inherent cyclicality means that the company's income statement performance can be volatile and subject to industry-wide trends.

There is a significant exposure to geopolitical risks, especially related to relationships between the United States and China. In 2023, the Chinese government restricted Micron's sales into data centers, which led to a significant dip in revenue (readers can see it on the above chart). The return of Donald Trump to the White House increases geopolitical risks for Micron because the trade war against China commenced during Donald Trump's first presidential term.

While I mentioned that Micron is successful in navigating a very competitive environment, this does not exclude significant competition risks. The presence of formidable competitors means that the margin for error is small, and Micron should constantly innovate and differentiate to protect its moat. This is a challenging target, that requires substantial inherently risky R&D investments.

The Verdict

Micron's stock looks extremely attractive, with a deep 56% discount as per my modeling analysis. There are certain risks, but investing in equities is inherently risky. The company is very strong, according to my analysis of internal factors. This makes MU well-positioned to capitalize on favorable external factors.

data.title: Enovix: A Worthy Buy For Asymmetric Upside
data.description:
Performance Assessment

Enovix Corporation (NASDAQ:ENVX) has lagged the S&P 500 Index (SP500, SPY, SPX, IVV, VOO) since my last update on the stock:

Thesis

The company reported its Q4 FY 2024 results a couple of days ago.

  1. Commercialization may be progressing slower than expected.
  2. Progress on customer engagements, operations and R&D is on track.
  3. Valuations are discounted to historical levels but without the tailwind of revenue upgrades.
  4. Relative technicals point bullish.
  5. Short interest movements are a key monitorable.

Commercialization May Be Progressing Slower Than Expected

In my last bullish note, I had stated the following:

The market may be underestimating the rapid commercial ramp potential.

However, I am starting to have doubts on this part of the thesis for 2 key reasons:

Firstly, actual revenue guidance has consistently been below consensus estimates. In Q4, there was a 28.6% miss. And looking at the consensus revisions for revenues over FY 2025's quarters, we broadly see net downgrade revisions:

Naturally, this is flowing down to the EBITDA level as well:

And this is an issue to monitor as the company is loss-making and burning FCF every quarter:

However, there is sufficient liquidity ($273 million) in the company for funds to last more than a year, assuming even $50 million/quarter cash burn.

Secondly and perhaps more importantly for the long term is the fact that management's disclosures on the throughput unit economics of their Malaysia fab is below prior expectations:

The current Line 1, Gen2 Line 1, which is installed in our Malaysia fab is running and was bought off in SAT [Site Acceptance Testing] at 1,350 UPH, just as a reminder, which is equivalent of roughly 9.5 million to 10 million batteries a year.

- COO Ajay Marathe in the Q4 FY 2024 earnings call.

This throughput of 1,350 units per hour is 18.2% lower than the 1,650 units per hour outlined in the Q3 FY 2024 Investor Presentation:

Now the company has maintained a 1,650 units per hour throughput as their longer-term target even in the Q4 FY 2024 update. But I was expecting this to be hit early on during the first high-volume manufacturing line's operation. So it is still a net downward revision vs. my prior expectations.

By the way, management's latest disclosures have allowed us to deduce some more important data points, such as implied revenue per battery unit used in smartphones ($12.59):

I am confident that this data will be useful to quantify the impact of various catalysts later on.

Progress On Customer Engagements, Operations, And R&D Is On Track

Despite slower than expected progress on commercialization of the business, I am still optimistic on the broader growth story, which I have discussed in more depth in my initiating coverage of Enovix.

I am pleased to see that the company is progressing well on various milestones:

Smartphone Customers

Enovix met its timelines to ship engineering samples to their lead smartphone OEM, with a pass on all critical safety tests. This means they are on-track to support the launches of multiple smartphone models with their batteries:

Also, Enovix expanded its footprint to include another smartphone OEM. This brings the total to 7 of the top 8 smartphone OEMs that make up more than 80% of global smartphone volumes:

On the IoT side, Enovix secured a prepaid purchase order from a global technology company for smart eyewear products. The first samples were already delivered to this customer earlier this month (Feb 2025). And it remains on track for initial shipments by mid-2025. Whilst I am unsure of the magnitude of this order, I do note that management described it as "sizable."

Manufacturing

As mentioned earlier, Enovix met its Q4 deadlines to get its first high-volume-manufacturing (HVM) line live to ship its first EX-2M samples. Management also claimed yield improvements but unfortunately did not give a specific number, making it harder to assess their progress toward 90% yields they had stated would be the viable range in Q2 FY 2024:

Essentially, this business makes sense when we get to very high 90 plus 95 plus percentage yields when we're in high volume manufacturing.

- CEO Raj Telluri in the Q2 FY 2024 earnings call.

Products

Enovix's EX-2M battery samples have been shipped on schedule and the company is now in the design phase of the next generation EX-3M battery that aims for even greater energy density and faster charging:

Takeaway

All in all, I think it is encouraging to see the company progress on its engagements with key customers, manufacturing operations and R&D in a timely way. This is not something to be taken for granted, as many companies often falter and extend their timelines (in my investing coverage, Solid Power (SLDP) is an example of this). I view this as a key ingredient of success for a company that is en-route to mass commercialization of its business model.

Valuations Are Discounted To Historical Levels But Without The Tailwind Of Revenue Upgrades

As Enovix's business has not yet reached stable revenue generating operations beyond smaller sampling related revenues, it is harder to value it on traditional metrics. It's 1-yr fwd EV/Revenues stands at 60.26x, which seems high but is much better than the 200x+ levels it was at before:

What is more interesting to me is how the component drivers of the share price are moving based on a split of the revenue estimates (gray line in the chart below) and the 1-yr fwd EV/Revenue valuation multiple (blue line in the chart below):

It looks like over H2 FY 2024, the revenue expectations index has remained broadly flat, which suggests a lack of a revenue upgrades tailwind currently. This reduces some confidence in my bullish outlook. However, I am wary of putting too much weight on this as I contend that what matters most for the company right now is how it progresses on its various customer, manufacturing, and R&D milestones.

Relative Technicals Point Bullish

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using Technical Analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder return as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

Relative Read of ENVX vs. SP500

ENVX vs. SP500 is stuck in a big weekly range since Aug. 2024. But since Dec. 2024, there has been strong bullish price action. Recently, the ratio prices have failed to break below a key support level, posting false breakouts and wick rejections to the downside. Hence, I believe we are likely to see some upside ahead, potentially leading to a breakout of the sideways range depending on how the fundamentals pan out.

Short Interest Movements Are A Key Monitorable

Being a cash burning company without a scalable business just yet, there are obviously many execution risks in this stock. The stock is also highly volatile with unusual trading behavior as noted by MassifCapital. One metric I am tracking is the company's short interest, as I think this can give a good gauge of the broader sentiment around the stock:

Currently, the short interest is 23.77%; below peak levels of 30% at the end of Q3 FY 2024. I view this as a positive, bullish sign.

Takeaway & Positioning

Enovix has not performed so well since my bullish initiating coverage on the company. I think its Q4 FY 2024 results indicate that revenue commercialization may be progressing slower than expected due to guidance misses vs. consensus and lower throughput in its first high-volume battery manufacturing line. But on the bright side, its progress on customer engagements, operations, and R&D is on track without any delays; a respectable achievement.

ENVX stock's valuations are still high because it's a largely pre-(scaled)-revenue company. However, I think the greater focus should be on how it progresses on its milestones. The relative technicals point bullish, although the stock has exhibited highly volatile behavior. To gauge the overall market sentiment, I am tracking the short interest movements. In this regard, I view it as a bullish sign that the short interest has dropped around 6 percentage points from its peak ~30% levels.

Overall, I rate the stock a "Buy."

How to interpret Hunting Alpha's ratings:

Strong Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P 500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence. I also have a net long position in the security in my personal portfolio.

Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P 500 on a total shareholder return basis

Neutral/hold: Expect the company to perform in-line with the S&P 500 on a total shareholder return basis

Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P 500 on a total shareholder return basis

Strong Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P 500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence

The typical time-horizon for my views is multiple quarters to more than a year. It is not set in stone. However, I will share updates on my changes in stance in a pinned comment to this article and may also publish a new article discussing the reasons for the change in view.
